headline: Amazon Coffee Farmers Find Profit in Forest Protection
meta_description: Ecuador's coffee farmers are exporting record amounts of deforestation-free coffee while protecting over 1,200 hectares of Amazon rainforest.
summary_short: Nearly 400 Ecuadorian coffee producers are proving that forest conservation and profitable farming can go hand in hand—with exports tripling in 2025.
body:
Victoria Alverca Peña has grown coffee in Ecuador's Zamora Chinchipe province for 25 years. But only recently has her work felt truly valued. "I take care of the environment, I don't cut down trees, and my coffee will be valued more highly," she says. "I'll be able to sell it under better conditions."

Alverca is part of a quiet revolution reshaping the Amazon's relationship with agriculture. Since 2019, nearly 400 coffee producers across the Ecuadorian rainforest have adopted a deforestation-free model combining satellite monitoring, traceability systems, and independent verification. The results have been striking: in 2025 alone, they exported as much deforestation-free coffee as during the entire previous three years combined.

Across nearly 5,000 hectares of farmland, more than 1,200 hectares of natural forest remain standing—a rare achievement in a region where agriculture has historically meant clearing trees. The farmers haven't sacrificed food security either. On their farms, alongside coffee plants, they grow cacao, fruit trees, and short-cycle crops like corn and cassava that nourish their families.

The initiative arrives at a critical moment. The European Union's strict new deforestation regulation takes effect soon, requiring proof that coffee imports weren't grown on recently cleared land. These Ecuadorian farmers are already ahead, demonstrating that protecting forests and earning decent incomes aren't opposing goals—they're partners.
